items.q: Why does light bend when it enters a denser medium like glass?
items.correct: Because it slows down — the change in speed at the boundary causes the wavefront to bend (towards the normal).
items.misconception: Because the medium is denser, it physically blocks part of the light and forces it to turn.
items.random_wrong: Because gravity in the glass pulls the light down.
items.why: Students attach refraction to 'density' as a material property without linking it to the change in wave speed and Snell's law.

items.q: What is the difference between nuclear fission and fusion?
items.correct: Fission splits a heavy nucleus into smaller ones; fusion joins light nuclei into a heavier one.
items.misconception: Fission and fusion are the same process, just at different scales.
items.random_wrong: Fission combines small atoms into bigger ones, and fusion splits them.
items.why: The words sound similar (both start 'fi-'), and students mix up which one combines and which one splits.

items.q: Is background radiation always present, even when no source is being studied?
items.correct: Yes — natural sources (rocks, cosmic rays, food) produce a low level of radiation everywhere on Earth.
items.misconception: No — background radiation only appears near nuclear power stations.
items.random_wrong: Yes, but only in cities with old buildings.
items.why: Students associate radiation exclusively with nuclear technology and don't realise natural background dose is ubiquitous.

items.q: Young's double slit experiment was first conducted in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1801. “1687” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Rayleigh resolution criterion coefficient for a circular aperture:
items.why: Correct answer: 1.22. “1.00” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year of Young's double slit experiment?
items.why: Exact answer: 1801 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
